files.content:
# COMP 321 Project 3: Linking

## Student Information
Name: Bebe  
NetID: bebe

## Problem 7.6
In `swap.c`, `buf` is declared `extern`, so it is resolved to the definition in `m.c` during linking. `bufp0` is initialized to `&buf[0]`, and `bufp1` is set to `&buf[1]` inside `swap()`. The function then exchanges the two values through pointer dereferences. The local static variable `count` inside `incr()` has internal linkage and static storage duration, so it persists across calls but is only visible within that function.

## Problem 7.9
`foo6.c` defines `main` as a function, while `bar6.c` defines `char main;` as an object with the same external symbol name. This creates a name collision at link/load time. In `p2()`, `printf("0x%x\n", main);` treats the object symbol as data and prints the low byte at the symbol location, which demonstrates how mixing code and data symbols with identical names is unsafe and can produce undefined or platform-specific behavior.

## Problem 7.12
Global symbols with external linkage are visible across translation units and can be resolved by the linker; file-scope `static` symbols have internal linkage and are not exported. Linkers resolve references by symbol name plus symbol type/binding rules, and multiple strong definitions of the same global symbol are a link-time error. This is why controlling linkage (`extern` vs `static`) is essential for avoiding collisions and unintended inter-module coupling.

## ReadJCF

### Testing Strategy
I implemented `readjcf` incrementally by filling one group of parser functions at a time: header validation, constant-pool parsing, class body/interfaces parsing, and attribute skipping. After implementing the first complete parsing pass, I ran the provided grade tool and used the failing/passing feedback to confirm edge cases such as bad tags, out-of-range indices, truncated files, extra trailing bytes, and special constant-pool cases (`Long`/`Double` two-slot entries and zero-length UTF-8 constants). I repeated this process until all tests passed and then reran the full grader to confirm stability.

## Two Things I Learned
1. GCC zero-length/flexible trailing arrays in packed structs are a common C pattern for variable-sized binary records. You allocate extra bytes beyond `sizeof(struct)` and then access the trailing payload through the array member.
2. `__attribute__((packed))` is important when reading file formats directly into structs, because default alignment/padding inserted by the compiler would otherwise make `fread` layouts mismatch the on-disk byte layout.

The function then exchanges the two values through pointer dereferences. The local static variable `count` inside `incr()` has internal linkage and static storage duration, so it persists across calls but is only visible within that function.

## Problem 7.9
`foo6.c` defines `main` as a function, while `bar6.c` defines `char main;` as an object with the same external symbol name. This creates a name collision at link/load time. In `p2()`, `printf("0x%x\n", main);` treats the object symbol as data and prints the low byte at the symbol location, which demonstrates how mixing code and data symbols with identical names is unsafe and can produce undefined or platform-specific behavior.

## ReadJCF

### Testing Strategy
I implemented `readjcf` incrementally by filling one group of parser functions at a time: header validation, constant-pool parsing, class body/interfaces parsing, and attribute skipping. After implementing the first complete parsing pass, I ran the provided grade tool and used the failing/passing feedback to confirm edge cases such as bad tags, out-of-range indices, truncated files, extra trailing bytes, and special constant-pool cases (`Long`/`Double` two-slot entries and zero-length UTF-8 constants). graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP321 Linking (readjcf) - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 2/5 problems (52%)
Points: 13/25

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[FAIL] Problem 1: Problem 7.6 (2/5 pts)
       Your response shows useful linkage understanding, but it does not fully answer the textbook-style 7.6 objective in the rubric. To earn full credit, explicitly classify relevant symbols as strong or weak and then step through the linker rules to reach the final chosen definitions/values. As written, the explanation is partially correct but incomplete relative to the required resolution-rule analysis.
       Errors: Does not apply the strong vs. weak symbol framework expected for this problem.; Does not explicitly use linker resolution rules (multiple strong error, strong over weak, weak/weak behavior).; Does not provide the final symbol-resolution outcome in the rubric's expected format.

[FAIL] Problem 2: Problem 7.9 (1/5 pts)
       This answer demonstrates general linking awareness, but rubric 7.9 specifically expects relocation mechanics and numeric computation. To improve, identify the relocation type, compute symbol/reference addresses, apply the correct relocation formula, and present the final relocated value in hex.
       Errors: Does not address relocation entries/types expected by the rubric (e.g., PC-relative vs absolute).; No relocation formula or address computation is shown.; No hexadecimal relocation result is derived.

[FAIL] Problem 3: Problem 7.12 (1/5 pts)
       Your explanation is technically correct about symbol visibility, but it does not answer the 7.12 focus: library ordering and archive member selection. Full credit requires tracing how the linker scans objects/libraries left-to-right and showing why specific orders succeed or fail.
       Errors: Does not describe the left-to-right library/archive scanning algorithm.; Does not trace unresolved/defined symbol sets during linking.; Does not analyze command-line order effects or circular dependency handling.

[PASS] Problem 4: READJCF Testing Strategy (4/5 pts)
       This is a solid, practical strategy with meaningful edge-case and failure-path coverage, and it aligns well with readjcf verification requirements. The main gap is breadth on positive/valid input variety and explicit output-validation methodology. Adding a matrix of representative valid class types plus byte-for-byte output comparison criteria would bring this to full credit.
       Errors: Limited detail on breadth of valid-class coverage (different class structures/compilers/modifiers).; No explicit output cross-check strategy (e.g., against reference output or javap-style validation).

[PASS] Problem 5: Two Things I Learned (5/5 pts)
       This section is strong and on target. Both items are concrete C-language/system-level features used in class-file parsing, and your explanations show clear understanding of why they matter in real binary I/O code.

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
You show solid practical understanding in the implementation/testing and C-features sections, especially around binary parsing pitfalls. The main issue is mismatch with the textbook-problem prompts: your 7.6/7.9/7.12 responses contain related linking concepts but do not address the specific required analyses (strong/weak rule application for 7.6, relocation computation for 7.9, and library order algorithm tracing for 7.12). Focus on explicitly following each problem’s expected method and final computed outcomes.

============================================================
